parcels.__comment: Read in the main parcel data file. This contains all of the parcel-level attributes from the Allegheny County real estate dataset.

sales.__comment: Read in the sales data file. This contains all of the sales information from the Allegheny County real estate dataset.

calc.__comment: key_sale is used as a unique id for sales - it consists of the parcel id plus the date
calc.__comment1: deed_id is used for valid sales detection. If we have multiple sales with the same deed number, they are multi-parcel sales and should be excluded. Multi parcel sales should already be excluded by looking at the SALECODE field in the county dataset, but this provides an extra layer of validation.
calc.__comment2: valid_sale is an important field. It will be set to 'true' only for typical sales, and false for multi-parcel sales, love and affection sales, etc. For modeling purposes, we will only use valid sales, since other sales may or may not reflect market rates.

geo_parcels.__comment: Load in the shapefile, which contains geometric data for every parcel. We need this for all location-based analysis.

market_value.__comment: Load in market value data from https://data.wprdc.org/dataset/market-value-analysis-2021 (not currently used in our models)

flood_zones.__comment: Load in flood zone data from https://data.wprdc.org/dataset/2014-fema-flood-zones (not currently used in our models)

undermined.__comment: Load in undermined data from https://data.wprdc.org/dataset/undermined-areas (not currently used in our models)

city_council_districts.__comment: Load in city council district from https://data.wprdc.org/dataset/city-council-districts-2012 (not currently used in our models)

county_council_districts.__comment: Load in county council district from https://openac-alcogis.opendata.arcgis.com/datasets/AlCoGIS::allegheny-county-council-districts (not currently used in our models)

process.__comment: These operations actually assemble your data. 'merge' will assemble the base dataframes from the input files, and 'enrich' will create additional calculated fields from the base data.

merge.__comment: universe contains intrinsic properties of each parcel. sales contains sales data.

dupes.__comment: Handle duplicates - this basically just deletes any instances where there are multiple rows for the same parcel.

invalid_sales.__comment: Second-pass scrub on top of the SALECODE='0 VALID SALE' flag. Allegheny valid improved sales have p1=$8,800; only ~0.12% sit below $5,000, and a 'valid'-coded house selling for <$5k is almost always bad data / a non-arms-length transfer the county code missed. Drop any sale under $1,000 and any improved sale under $5,000 (vacant land can legitimately be cheap, so it is exempt from the $5k floor). The third clause is a RELATIVE rule: an improved sale closing below half the assessor's fair-market total (FAIRMARKETTOTAL) is almost always distressed / non-arms-length. assr_market_value > 0 guards against parcels with no assessed total. Thresholds are conservative and tunable.

calc.__comment: sale_to_assr_ratio = sale_price / assr_market_value, using zero-safe division (/0 yields 0 when the assessor total is 0/blank, which the assr_market_value>0 guard in the filter excludes from the relative rule).

enrich.__comment1: Join the geometric categories onto the universe.
enrich.__comment_dem: USGS 3DEP elevation. Adds elevation_mean_ft, elevation_stdev_ft, slope_mean_deg.
enrich.__comment_distances: Proximity to rivers, other water, and parks (OSM-sourced). proximity_to_* saturates to 0 past max_distance and is the regression-friendly form. Rivers get a wider 2-mile radius; water/parks 1 mile.
enrich.__comment_census: Block-group demographics for Allegheny County (FIPS 42003): median_income, median_c_rent, median_g_rent, total_population. Requires a US Census API key in .env.
enrich.__comment2: spatial_lag computes weighted average of nearby (with a half mile) parcels. This is computed for a few different fields, including sale prices, building quality, building condition, and building age.

model_groups.__comment: The spatial_lag fields for residential will be based off the values of nearby residential parcels. Single-family is split into Pittsburgh (urban) and suburban submarkets, so each draws its spatial lag from its own submarket (urban rowhouse prices differ sharply from suburban detached). We are also using nearby residential data for commercial parcels, due to low amounts of data for commercial.

spatial_lag.__comment: List of fields to compute spatial lag with, alongside a number, indicating the max number of neighboring parcels to include in the weighted average

fill.__comment: This fills any blanks in the dataset with defaults. Different fields can use different defaults, and we can also split up the behavior for vacant vs. improved parcels.
fill.__comment_median_enrichment: Enrichment numerics are imputed to their median for the small fraction of parcels the enrichment couldn't cover (census block-group misses ~3%, DEM coverage gaps ~1%). Required because mra (statsmodels OLS) rejects any NaN in the design matrix; proximity_to_* and footprint are already 0-filled by their enrichers so they're not listed here.

try_variables.__comment: try_variables is just used for exploratory analysis. The pipeline will compute R^2 and other statistics for each variable, and tell you which ones are best.

metadata.__comment: Training reaches back to 2022 (post-2020 market regime); only sales since 2025 are used for testing / ratio studies (IAAO 1-year window).
metadata.__comment_use_sales_from: Per-model-group windows: 'default' applies to every group; 'by_model_group' overrides. The big suburban-rest group floods (~5.4k valid sales/yr) so it gets the leanest 2023 window (still ~16k sales); the smaller SF groups keep 2022. Commercial is data-starved (only ~670 valid sales 2016-2025 countywide) so it reaches back to 2016 to scrape together enough.

instructions.__comment: This is where the models actually get run to generate predictions. We run a few different models and combine them to get the final assessments.
instructions.__comment2: 'assessor' just takes the existing assessments (this is just included for comparison purposes and will not be included in the final predictions). 'mra' is a standard linear regression. 'multi_mra' runs linear regressions within subsets of the data (breakdowns by census tract, municipality, etc) and combines them into a single estimate. 'local_area' and 'spatial_lag_area' are simple models that try to predict values based on nearby sale prices per square foot. 'lcomp', 'lightgbm', 'xgboost', 'ngboost' and 'catboost' are machine learning decision tree models. 'gwr' is gradient-weighted regression.

main.__comment_ensemble: Blend the run models (excluding 'assessor', which is comparison-only) into a single ensemble.

models.__comment: This is where we choose which variables to include when running the models.

main.__comment_ind_vars: ind_vars feed the linear (mra/multi_mra) models; the nearest-neighbor models (local_area/spatial_lag_area) ignore them. Lists are per-group.

commercial.__comment_trees: Tree/ensemble models (gradient-boosted xgboost/lightgbm/catboost, plus ngboost which also yields a per-parcel prediction_std). All share the wide kitchen-sink ind_vars_tree list (same as lcomp) - trees tolerate redundancy/collinearity and NaNs, so no fill rules or lean lists needed; they stay in PRICE space (no log flag - trees do not extrapolate negative). n_trials is the Optuna search budget per model (deterministic + crash-resumable). catboost is by far the slowest (CPU-only here for reproducibility; GPU catboost is non-deterministic) - lower its n_trials or remove it if iteration gets slow.

residential_single_family_suburban_prewa.__comment_log: multi_mra trains on LOG(price) for this group (log:true) so the linear models cannot predict negative values for expensive/atypical homes; each model exponentiates its own predictions back to price space, so metrics/ensemble are unaffected. Other engines (assessor/local_area/spatial_lag_area/lcomp) ignore this flag and stay in price space. To turn off, set log:false or remove these entries.
residential_single_family_suburban_prewa.__comment: Suburban pre-war (inner-ring boroughs, ~1920 stock): condition/renovation- and location-driven.

residential_single_family_suburban.__comment: Suburban post-war detached (the bulk, ~1960 stock): size-driven, more uniform.

prediction.__comment: Where each published value comes from. market_value = the 'main' ensemble (the mean/median blend of the run models).

model_groups.__comment: Defining the model groups. Each group will be modelled separately.
model_groups.__comment_sf_split: Single-family is split via is_pittsburgh_sd into the City of Pittsburgh school district (urban, rowhouse/townhouse-heavy, ~104k) vs the surrounding suburbs (~376k). Suburban is split again on building typology: pre-war detached stock (STYLE '16 OLD STYLE', median year built 1920, ~$48/sqft) is carved out from the rest (post-war detached + attached + vacant, ~$94/sqft). The pre-war cut is justified empirically: STYLE is 99.97% populated and OLD STYLE is 98% built before 1945, and the two eras are strongly regionally segregated (83% of suburban detached sit in neighborhoods that are >=70% one era; inner-ring boroughs ~65-76% old vs outer suburbs ~2-4% old). The 'suburban' group is the residual (everything suburban NOT pre-war-detached)

analysis.__comment: Ratio studies will break down the sales by value and size bucket, as well as by location/municipality. This can be used to spot specific sets of parcels for which we have bad predictions.
analysis.__comment_equity: land_equity / impr_equity come from the settings template (which omits 'location'); give them a location so the equity clusters group by neighborhood, matching horizontal_equity and sales_scrutiny.

field_classification.__comment: important fields are used for breakdowns in ratio study reports
field_classification.__comment1: This section indicates the data type for each variable, as well as whether it's a characteristic of the land or of the building (this distinction is mainly relevant for the vacant land analysis module, which we are not currently using)

ref.__comment_ind_vars_tree: Wide 'kitchen-sink' ind_vars for the tree/ensemble archetype (lcomp/xgboost/lightgbm/catboost/ngboost if enabled). Trees tolerate redundancy/collinearity, so this layers the string ordinal grades, the full spatial_lag suite, and extra census fields on top of what the lean linear 'default' uses. Deliberately NO high-cardinality location categoricals (neighborhood/census_tract/municipality): location comes from lat/long + spatial_lag_sale_price, which is safe for one-hot engines like lcomp. 'undermined' is included but is informative only for the Pittsburgh urban group (constant elsewhere -> trees ignore it).

filters.__comment: These are the definitions for our model groups
